intro.title: Quantifiers: some, any, much, many, all, etc.

sections.title: What are quantifiers?
sections.content:
**Quantifiers** tell us **how much** or **how many**. They come before nouns:

• **some** water, **many** books, **all** students

The right quantifier depends on two things:
• Is the noun **countable** or **uncountable**?
• Is the sentence **positive**, **negative**, or a **question**?

sections.title: Some and any
sections.content:
**Some** — positive statements, offers, and requests: *I have some milk. Would you like some tea?*
**Any** — questions and negatives: *Do you have any milk? I don't have any money.*
**Some in questions** when we expect 'yes' (offers): *Would you like some cake? Can I have some water?*

sections.title: Much and many
sections.content:
**Much** — uncountable nouns (often in questions/negatives): *How much water? I don't have much time.*
**Many** — countable nouns (often in questions/negatives): *How many books? There aren't many people.*
In positive statements, we often use **a lot of** instead: *I have a lot of time.* (not: *I have much time.*)

sections.title: A lot of, lots of, enough
sections.content:
**A lot of / lots of** — both countable and uncountable; mainly positive: *A lot of people. Lots of milk.*
**Enough** — sufficient; before a noun: *enough time, enough money.* After an adjective: *old enough, big enough.* *We have enough water. She's old enough to drive.*

sections.title: All, most, no, none
sections.content:
**All** — the whole amount or group: *all the water, all students.*
**Most** — the majority: *most people, most of the time.*
**No** — zero (positive verb): *I have no time* = *I don't have any time.*
**None** — not any (used without a noun): *None of it. None left.*

sections.title: Few, a few, little, a little
sections.content:
**Few / a few** — with countable nouns: **Few** = not many (negative): *Few people came.* (almost nobody). **A few** = some (positive): *I have a few friends.* (a small number).
**Little / a little** — with uncountable nouns: **Little** = not much (negative): *Little progress was made.* **A little** = some (positive): *I need a little help.*

sections.title: Typical errors (1)
sections.content:
• **Any in positive:** Wrong: *I have any milk.* Right: *I have **some** milk.*
• **Much + countable:** Wrong: *much books.* Right: ***many** books.*
• **Many + uncountable:** Wrong: *many water.* Right: ***much** water.*

sections.title: Typical errors (2)
sections.content:
• **Few vs a few:** *Few people came* = almost nobody. *A few people came* = some people (positive).
• **Little vs a little:** *Little hope* = almost no hope. *A little hope* = some hope (positive).

sections.title: Reference
sections.content: For more on countable and uncountable nouns, see the Uncountable Nouns topic under Choose Grammar Topic.

questions.question: 'I have _____ milk.' (positive) Which fits?
'How _____ books did you read?' Which fits?
'She has _____ friends.' (a small number, positive) Which fits?
'There isn't _____ time.' Which fits?
'I need _____ help.' (a small amount, positive) Which fits?
I have _____ milk in the fridge.
Do you have _____ sugar?
How _____ people were at the party?
She has _____ friends in London.
There is _____ water left. (almost none)
We have _____ time to finish. (some)
_____ the students passed the exam.
I don't have _____ money.
There are _____ apples on the table. (a small number)
He has _____ experience in teaching.
questions.explanation: Positive statement, uncountable → a lot of.

questions.question: Wrong: 'I have any milk.' Correct the sentence.
Wrong: 'How much books did you read?' Correct the sentence.
answers: How many books did you read?
How many books did you read

questions.question: Wrong: 'She has few friends.' (meaning she has some friends) Correct the sentence.
Wrong: 'I need a little eggs.' Correct the sentence.
Wrong: 'There isn't some bread.' Correct the sentence.
Wrong: 'How many water do you need?' Correct the sentence.
answers: How much water do you need?
How much water do you need

questions.question: Wrong: 'Less and less people are using public transport.' Correct the sentence.
answers: Fewer and fewer people are using public transport.
Fewer and fewer people are using public transport
questions.explanation: People is countable → use **fewer**, not **less**. **Less** is for uncountable nouns (less time, less water); **fewer** is for countable nouns (fewer people, fewer books).
